patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 4-year-old boy. Speak very little; let your parent answer most questions. Act anxious (fidget, cling, quiet voice). Start by saying: “My joints feel really stiff and my hands are getting claw-like, and I get more short of breath when I walk.” If asked what you notice, add: morning stiffness, harder to bend fingers, dropping toys, winded on stairs, and you were told your urine had “extra sugars” but “not diabetes.” Only share other problems if directly asked: frequent ear infections/muffled hearing, constant stuffy nose/mouth breathing, loud snoring/waking up gasping, tight shoulders/hips, night hand tingling, rounded belly/early fullness, weak teeth/cavities, light sensitivity/blurry vision. Do not mention hidden issues unless the clinician asks specifically about growth, fatigue, joints not straightening, heart flutter/lightheadedness, tight finger skin, or constipation. If asked about mood/teasing, admit you feel sad/anxious and avoid people. Do not bring up sexual pain unless explicitly asked (then be embarrassed and reluctant). Express misconceptions: worry it’s rheumatoid arthritis, asthma, pinched nerve, or “just sleep apnea.” If the clinician shows empathy, nod, relax slightly, and say “okay” or “I’m scared,” and ask if testing will cost a lot or affect insurance.

Urine oligosaccharides (TLC/LC-MS screen.value: Increased (pattern consistent with lysosomal storage disorder)

Plasma lysosomal enzymes (multiple) - ex.value: Elevated (e.g., beta-hexosaminidase 2.8x ULN; arylsulfatase A 2.1x ULN)

Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) 1 drop OU QID
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO divided BID
confounders: Oculocerebrodental (Lowe) syndrome can cause developmental delay, hypotonia, cataracts, and renal tubular dysfunction that overlap with lysosomal storage disease manifestations
Recurrent upper airway infections/adenoid hypertrophy causing noisy breathing and mild tachypnea, mimicking storage-disease-related airway involvement
Iron deficiency anemia contributing to tachycardia and fatigue, potentially attributed to chronic disease
Subclinical hypothyroidism contributing to poor growth and constipation, overlapping with storage disorder symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis type II (I-cell disease) / mucolipidosis type III spectrum due to GNPTAB-related lysosomal targeting defect
required_evidence: Urine glycosaminoglycans (GAG) screen showing absent or only mildly increased GAGs despite mucopolysacchariduria-like phenotype
Urine oligosaccharides and/or urine sialyl-oligosaccharides showing increased oligosaccharides consistent with a glycoproteinosis/lysosomal trafficking disorder
Plasma lysosomal enzymes panel showing markedly elevated extracellular activities of multiple lysosomal enzymes (misrouting due to defective mannose-6-phosphate tagging)
Confirmatory GNPTAB sequencing consistent with mucolipidosis II/III

expected_lab_results.Urine glycosaminoglycans (GAG) screen: Normal or mildly elevated (not the marked elevation typical of mucopolysaccharidoses); may be reported as negative/borderline depending on assay
expected_lab_results.Urine oligosaccharides (TLC/LC-MS screen: Abnormal with increased oligosaccharides consistent with a glycoproteinosis/lysosomal storage disorder (pattern supportive of ML II/III rather than isolated MPS)
expected_lab_results.Urine sialyl-oligosaccharides: Increased sialylated oligosaccharides (supports lysosomal storage disorder with glycoprotein catabolism/trafficking defect)
expected_lab_results.Plasma lysosomal enzymes (multiple) - ex: Markedly elevated activities of multiple lysosomal hydrolases in plasma/serum (classic for ML II/III due to enzyme secretion)
expected_lab_results.I-cell disease/ML II/III confirmatory te: Pathogenic/likely pathogenic biallelic GNPTAB variants consistent with ML II or ML III (phenotype-dependent)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may show mild anemia depending on chronic disease/nutrition
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - MCV: Often normal; can be low if iron deficiency or high if other comorbidity (not diagnostic)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - WBC: Often normal; may be elevated with intercurrent infection (not diagnostic)

must_include: Working diagnosis is mucolipidosis (ML II/III spectrum), a rare inherited lysosomal storage disorder
Key mechanism: enzymes are made but misdirected outside cells due to defective mannose-6-phosphate tagging, leading to buildup inside lysosomes
Why tests matter: urine screening plus plasma enzyme pattern and GNPTAB sequencing confirm the diagnosis
Differentiate from mucopolysaccharidoses: similar clinical features can occur, but urine GAGs are often not markedly elevated in ML II/III
No curative therapy in routine practice; care focuses on symptom control and monitoring complications
Multidisciplinary plan (cardiac, airway/ENT, ortho, therapy services) to preserve function and quality of life
Set expectations about prognosis varying by ML II vs ML III and by severity; emphasize individualized care plan
Discuss inheritance (autosomal recessive) and recurrence risk for siblings/future pregnancies
Offer genetic counseling and family testing options
Acknowledge uncertainty until confirmatory results return and outline next steps and timeline
Infection prevention and prompt evaluation for respiratory symptoms
Supportive therapies at home (mobility aids, safe activity, feeding strategies if needed)
Importance of keeping specialty follow-ups and surveillance testing (echo, hearing/vision, airway assessment)

Review results to support ML II/III spectrum and exclude closer mimics (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses).
State working diagnosis as mucolipidosis (ML II/III spectrum) with rationale and differential, and document level of certainty pending confirmatory sequencing if not yet available.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/mechanism/testing rationale and differentiation from MPS; explain treatment as supportive multidisciplinary care with prognosis expectations; address inheritance/recurrence risk and genetic counseling; provide lifestyle and surveillance guidance.
Arrange genetics/metabolic follow-up and referrals for multidisciplinary surveillance (cardiology/echo, ENT/airway, ortho, PT/OT/SLP, audiology/vision) and set a timeline for pending results review.
If any medication is being considered/prescribed (e.g., antibiotics for infection, analgesics), perform an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety requirement).
If prescribing, check for interactions with current meds/supplements (noncritical safety check).
Only if clinically indicated for symptoms/complications (e.g., infection treatment, pain control); avoid implying disease-modifying therapy is available in routine practice.
Close after ensuring understanding, providing return precautions (especially respiratory), and confirming follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male IT support specialist. Open with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches, mostly at night.” Voluntarily describe severe stabbing pain behind the left eye in sudden attacks, rapid ramp-up, unbearable intensity, waking you from sleep, left eye tearing/redness, left nostril runny/stuffy, and that you can’t sit still (pace/rock). Add that it happens in clusters for a few weeks, then months of relief.

If asked, give details: attacks last 45–60 minutes and stop abruptly; occur around the same time nightly; no nausea; light/sound not a big issue; alcohol triggers during cluster weeks; oxygen helped once; father had “suicide headaches.”

Withhold unless asked: pre-cluster twinges, droopy eyelid/smaller pupil, panic/agitation at onset, heavy OTC painkiller use, smoking and worse clusters when smoking more.

Be resistant about increased drinking and passive thoughts (“can’t live with this pain”)—admit only if directly and gently asked; clarify no intent to self-harm.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s sinus infection or trigeminal neuralgia due to occasional electric zings with touch/chewing.

Mention cost/job worries when discussing tests, oxygen, or referrals. If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed at headache onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain
Medication-overuse headache risk due to frequent NSAID use during headache bouts
Alcohol as a trigger during active cluster periods (patient reports attacks reliably after beer/wine)
Active tobacco use (1 pack/day), associated with cluster headache and may bias history toward sinus-related complaints
Intermittent nasal congestion/rhinorrhea during attacks mimicking acute sinusitis

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial), episodic or chronic subtype to be determined
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day during a cluster period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation
Stereotyped attacks with circadian/circannual periodicity and family history supportive
No features strongly favoring trigeminal neuralgia (e.g., electric-shock pain lasting seconds, clear trigger zones, pain-free refractory periods between brief paroxysms)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal renal function expected (important if considering lithium or frequent NSAID use)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min nonrebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes PRN attacks
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour if needed (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg PO three times daily initially; titrate every 7-14 days as needed/tolerated (typical effective total 240-480 mg/day) with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge while preventive therapy takes effect)
If verapamil ineffective/contraindicated: Lithium carbonate 300 mg PO twice daily (titrate to serum level 0.4-0.8 mmol/L) with renal/thyroid monitoring
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, autonomic symptoms, triggers, response to therapy)
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist; consider occipital nerve block if refractory
Assess for secondary causes/red flags and consider neuroimaging if atypical features/new onset/abnormal neuro exam
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response and titrate preventive therapy; sooner (48-72 hours) if uncontrolled attacks or adverse effects

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe one-sided attacks
Autonomic symptoms/restlessness are typical and help distinguish it from trigeminal neuralgia
Familial tendency can occur; not caused by penicillin allergy
Red flags that require urgent reassessment (new neurologic deficits, sudden worst headache, fever/neck stiffness, new onset after age 50, persistent atypical symptoms)
Two-part approach: acute abortive therapy (oxygen, triptan) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and short bridge (steroid)
How to use oxygen correctly (nonrebreather, high flow, early at onset)
Triptan limits and key risks (avoid if significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease; do not exceed daily max)
Verapamil requires ECG monitoring and slow titration; watch for constipation, dizziness, bradycardia
Discuss expected timeline: preventives may take days to weeks; bridge helps short term
Safety planning for frequent attacks and when to seek urgent care
Review medication access and practical barriers (home oxygen supply, injection technique)
Avoid alcohol during a cluster bout
Sleep regularity and trigger tracking
Do not overuse acute medications to avoid complications; coordinate follow-up for titration

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: timing (15–180 min), frequency/day, circadian pattern, unilateral orbital/temporal location, severity, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis), agitation/restlessness, triggers (alcohol), prior similar bouts, family history, and red flags (thunderclap, fever/neck stiffness, neuro deficits, age >50 new onset).

Commit to likely diagnosis (familial episodic cluster headache) vs key differentials (migraine, trigeminal neuralgia) based on short, very severe unilateral attacks with autonomic symptoms and restlessness.
Perform allergy check before prescribing (non-critical but required); clarify allergies and reactions, including that penicillin allergy does not cause cluster headache.

Critical interaction check before prescribing triptans/verapamil/steroids (e.g., triptan + ergotamine within 24h; triptan + MAOI; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin; CYP3A4 inhibitors increasing verapamil; lithium interactions if considered).

Critical contraindication screen prior to ordering/prescribing: cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine history, pregnancy considerations, severe hepatic disease, arrhythmia/heart block/bradycardia, current meds (ergots, MAOIs), and oxygen safety (smoking/fire risk, severe COPD/CO2 retention risk).
Order ECG prior to verapamil initiation/titration (counts as ordering step requiring contraindication check beforehand); consider baseline vitals and cardiac history documentation.
Review ECG for PR prolongation/AV block/bradycardia before starting or escalating verapamil.

Initiate evidence-based two-part therapy: acute abortive (high-flow oxygen via nonrebreather; triptan) plus preventive (verapamil with slow titration and ECG monitoring) and short bridge (oral steroid taper) when appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (including red flags), explain treatment use/risk/limits, address concerns (timeline, access barriers), and lifestyle counseling (avoid alcohol during bout, sleep regularity, trigger tracking, avoid overuse).
Arrange follow-up for verapamil titration and ECG recheck, reassess attack frequency/response, and safety planning for escalation/urgent symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Agent recommends acute therapy with correct oxygen instructions: 100% oxygen via nonrebreather mask at high flow, started early at onset, typically for ~15–20 minutes, with attention to fire safety and smoking avoidance.

Agent recommends a triptan option appropriate for cluster headache (e.g., subcutaneous sumatriptan or intranasal zolmitriptan) and states key limits/risks: avoid in significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease; do not exceed daily maximum; avoid combination with ergot within 24 hours and other relevant interactions.

Agent recommends preventive therapy with verapamil and states need for ECG monitoring and slow titration; counsels on common adverse effects (constipation, dizziness, bradycardia) and when to seek care (syncope, palpitations).
Agent discusses a short-term bridge option (e.g., corticosteroid taper) and sets expectations that preventives may take days to weeks, while the bridge helps short term.

Agent addresses practical barriers: arranging home oxygen supply, teaching injection or nasal spray technique, and ensuring access/coverage; also provides safety planning for frequent attacks and when to seek urgent care.

Agent provides lifestyle education: avoid alcohol during a cluster bout, maintain regular sleep, track triggers, and avoid overuse of acute medications; coordinates follow-up for titration and monitoring.
Agent does not prescribe contraindicated medications if contraindications/interactions are present; instead offers safe alternatives and documents rationale.
Agent uses clear, patient-centered language and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent) before ending.

Confirmatory genetic testing: pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant in KMT2D (autosomal dominant) or KDM6A (X-linked)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
acceptable_alternatives: Secondary trigeminal neuralgia (due to structural lesion such as tumor or multiple sclerosis)
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief (seconds to 2 minutes) paroxysms of severe, unilateral facial pain in a trigeminal nerve distribution (V2/V3 most common)
Pain described as electric shock-like, stabbing, or shooting
Triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind) with refractory periods
No objective neurologic deficit on exam (if present, raises concern for secondary cause)
No signs of dental infection, sinusitis, or temporal arteritis explaining symptoms

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal at baseline (no leukocytosis). Monitor for leukopenia/agranulocytosis after starting carbamazepine.
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal at baseline. Monitor for anemia as a potential adverse effect.
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal at baseline. Monitor for thrombocytopenia as a potential adverse effect.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be normal at baseline; carbamazepine can cause hyponatremia (SIADH), so monitor for decreasing sodium after initiation/titration.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal; not expected to be directly affected by carbamazepine.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Typically normal; may track with sodium if hyponatremia develops.
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal; used as general baseline renal status though carbamazepine is primarily hepatically metabolized.

non_pharmacological: Identify and minimize triggers (chewing on affected side, cold wind, tactile triggers) while maintaining oral hygiene

If atypical features or neurologic deficits: obtain MRI brain with and without contrast focusing on trigeminal nerve to evaluate for secondary causes (tumor, MS, vascular compression) and refer to neurology
If medication-refractory or intolerant: discuss referral for procedural options (microvascular decompression, percutaneous rhizotomy, stereotactic radiosurgery)

must_include: Trigeminal neuralgia is due to irritation/misfiring of the trigeminal nerve causing brief, severe shock-like facial pain
Typical triggers and the episodic nature with pain-free intervals
Red flags that suggest a secondary cause (numbness, weakness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, onset at young age) and would prompt imaging/neurology evaluation
Carbamazepine is first-line and often effective; dosing is started low and titrated
Common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness) and safety precautions (avoid driving until effects known)

Serious adverse effects requiring urgent care: rash (concern for SJS/TEN), fever/sore throat/infection symptoms (possible low WBC), unusual bruising/bleeding (platelets), severe fatigue/pallor (anemia), confusion/seizures (hyponatremia)
Confirm allergies (penicillin causes urticarial rash; codeine causes nausea/vomiting) and clarify no known carbamazepine allergy
Discuss pregnancy/contraception considerations if applicable (carbamazepine is teratogenic and induces metabolism of hormonal contraceptives; advise effective contraception and counseling)

Review and reconcile current medications/supplements to avoid interactions (CYP inducers/inhibitors; alcohol; grapefruit; warfarin/DOAC considerations; macrolides/azoles can raise carbamazepine levels)
Avoid alcohol and other sedatives when starting/titrating due to additive CNS depression
Maintain hydration and report symptoms of low sodium (headache, nausea, confusion)
Keep a pain/trigger diary to guide titration and assess response

Medication reconciliation (prescribed/OTC/herbals), alcohol/grapefruit use, pregnancy status/contraception, and baseline comorbidities relevant to carbamazepine safety (liver disease, hyponatremia risk, hematologic history).

Communication milestone: explain diagnosis with episodic nature/triggers and red flags that would prompt imaging/neurology evaluation.
Order baseline safety labs for carbamazepine (CBC with differential/platelets, CMP including LFTs and sodium; consider pregnancy test if applicable).
Review baseline labs (ensure no significant cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, or hyponatremia before starting; address abnormalities).
Required safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies and specifically assess for prior carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/aromatic anticonvulsant reaction; document allergy details.
Required safety check before prescribing: assess for major interactions (macrolides/azoles, grapefruit, warfarin/DOACs, hormonal contraceptives, other CNS depressants, enzyme inducers/inhibitors).

Contraindication screen before prescribing: pregnancy/trying to conceive, severe hepatic disease, significant bone marrow suppression history, prior serious cutaneous adverse reaction; ensure no red-flag neuro deficits requiring urgent imaging first.
Initiate first-line therapy (carbamazepine) with low starting dose and titration plan; provide PRN guidance and precautions.

Communication milestone: explain treatment (expected benefit, titration, common side effects, driving precautions) and serious adverse effects requiring urgent care (SJS/TEN rash, infection signs, bruising/bleeding, anemia symptoms, hyponatremia symptoms).

Communication milestones: address concerns (allergies clarified; pregnancy/contraception counseling; interaction avoidance) and lifestyle education (avoid alcohol/sedatives, hydration/low sodium symptoms, pain/trigger diary).
Arrange follow-up for titration and monitoring (symptom response, adverse effects, repeat labs if indicated) and specify return precautions/red flags.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding and safety-netting.

nl_assertions: Asks targeted questions confirming paroxysmal, brief, severe shock-like facial pain in a trigeminal distribution with identifiable triggers (touch, chewing, brushing teeth, cold wind) and pain-free intervals.

Explicitly screens for red flags suggesting secondary trigeminal neuralgia or alternative diagnosis: facial numbness or sensory loss, weakness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, hearing/visual changes, constitutional symptoms, history of cancer/infection, and young age at onset; recommends imaging/neurology evaluation when present.
Differential is considered (dental pathology, TMJ disorder, sinusitis, cluster headache, postherpetic neuralgia) and reasons for/against are briefly stated based on the history.

Clarifies allergy details provided (penicillin: urticarial rash; codeine: nausea/vomiting) and confirms no known carbamazepine allergy; does not incorrectly label codeine intolerance as anaphylaxis or vice versa.

Reconciles current medications/supplements and specifically addresses interaction risks: macrolides/azoles and grapefruit (raise carbamazepine levels), enzyme inducers/inhibitors, alcohol/sedatives (additive CNS depression), and anticoagulants (warfarin/DOAC considerations).

Addresses pregnancy/contraception when applicable: states carbamazepine teratogenicity, enzyme induction reducing hormonal contraceptive efficacy, and recommends effective contraception and counseling; avoids prescribing without addressing pregnancy status when relevant.

Orders and reviews appropriate baseline labs before starting carbamazepine (CBC with differential/platelets and CMP with LFTs and sodium; pregnancy test if applicable) and responds appropriately to abnormal results (e.g., defers/adjusts therapy and escalates care).

Provides serious adverse effect counseling with urgent-care instructions: rash (SJS/TEN), fever/sore throat/infection signs (low WBC), unusual bruising/bleeding (platelets), severe fatigue/pallor (anemia), confusion/seizures (hyponatremia).
Lifestyle counseling includes: avoid alcohol/other sedatives during initiation/titration, maintain hydration and recognize low sodium symptoms, and keep a pain/trigger diary to guide titration.

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to a glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (ALG14/ALG2/DPAGT1 spectrum)
required_evidence: History of fatigable weakness (ocular/bulbar/limb) with onset in infancy/childhood and no fluctuating autoimmune features
Worsening with infections/exertion and improvement with rest (typical neuromuscular junction fatigability pattern)
No exposure-driven explanation (e.g., no aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, or macrolides started immediately before onset) though macrolides may worsen symptoms

Prior/expected neuromuscular testing consistent with postsynaptic transmission defect (e.g., decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG) and/or genetic confirmation of glycosylation-pathway CMS
Autoimmune myasthenia gravis less likely (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK antibodies if available, lack of thymic disease; not required if genetic diagnosis already known)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected); may be normal range unless intercurrent illness
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal; may be elevated if concurrent infection/physiologic stress precipitating weakness
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal; may be low if hyperventilation or high if chronic hypoventilation (not expected without respiratory failure)
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Normal (used to assess hydration/renal function before medication choices)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30-60 mg PO every 4-6 hours while awake (titrate to effect and tolerability; avoid excessive dosing if cramps/diarrhea/bronchial secretions)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO 3-4 times daily (titrate; typical max 80 mg/day; avoid if seizure history or significant QT risk)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or equivalent age/weight-appropriate dosing; monitor heart rate and tremor)
Epinephrine autoinjector is not indicated solely for CMS; do not prescribe unless separate anaphylaxis risk exists
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission, especially macrolides (e.g., azithromycin) given prior severe worsening; avoid aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, and neuromuscular blocking agents when possible

Respiratory monitoring during exacerbations: assess for bulbar symptoms, work of breathing, and consider bedside spirometry (FVC/NIF) if available; escalate to emergency care if respiratory compromise
Vaccination and infection prevention strategies to reduce triggers; prompt evaluation for infections that precipitate weakness
Physical therapy/energy conservation strategies; pacing and rest to manage fatigability
Referral/follow-up with neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for confirmation of subtype and long-term management
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up within 2-4 weeks after medication initiation/titration (earlier within 48-72 hours if significant bulbar/respiratory symptoms or rapid worsening)

must_include: Condition is an inherited neuromuscular junction disorder (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis) causing fatigable weakness
Glycosylation-pathway defects can impair neuromuscular transmission leading to fluctuating symptoms
Symptoms can worsen with illness, exertion, and certain medications
Goal is symptomatic improvement of neuromuscular transmission (pyridostigmine, amifampridine, and/or beta-agonist depending on subtype/response)
Dosing is titrated to benefit while monitoring side effects (GI effects with pyridostigmine; seizures/QT risk with amifampridine; tachycardia/tremor with albuterol)
Not all CMS subtypes respond to the same drugs; treatment may be adjusted based on response and specialist guidance
Explicitly acknowledge azithromycin allergy/intolerance manifested by worsened weakness and shortness of breath; advise to avoid macrolides and inform all clinicians
Penicillin allergy history (urticarial rash) should be documented; discuss alternative antibiotics if needed
Red flags requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, trouble swallowing/handling secretions, rapidly worsening weakness
Medication avoidance list for neuromuscular weakness (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium; caution with sedatives and muscle relaxants)
Energy conservation and pacing; plan activities with rest periods
Infection prevention and early treatment to prevent exacerbations

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, fatigability pattern, bulbar/respiratory involvement, triggers (illness/exertion/meds), functional impact, prior testing/diagnosis of CMS subtype, current meds and prior responses, and full allergy history.

If diagnosis/subtype is not already confirmed, order confirmatory/supportive evaluation (e.g., genetic testing panel for CMS/glycosylation genes, electrodiagnostics with repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fiber EMG, CK, basic labs to assess comorbidity) and to inform subtype-directed therapy.
Review results to support CMS with glycosylation defect and exclude key mimics/complications; confirm subtype where possible before selecting therapy.
State working/confirmed diagnosis: congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation-pathway defect; differentiate from autoimmune myasthenia gravis.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm azithromycin intolerance/allergy (worsened weakness/SOB) and penicillin urticarial rash; document and plan avoidance/alternatives.

Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interacting QT-prolonging drugs, seizure-threshold lowering meds, beta-agonist interactions, and agents that worsen NMJ transmission (e.g., magnesium, certain antibiotics).

Initiate or adjust subtype-appropriate symptomatic therapy (pyridostigmine and/or amifampridine and/or beta-agonist such as albuterol) with titration plan and monitoring parameters; avoid contraindicated/worsening agents.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (inherited NMJ disorder; glycosylation defects), explain treatment options and side effects/monitoring, address allergy/antibiotic concerns and red flags, and provide lifestyle/medication avoidance guidance.
Arrange neuromuscular specialist follow-up, reassessment of response/side effects, and earlier review if bulbar/respiratory symptoms worsen.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, red flags, and follow-up.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 72-year-old man, anxious but able to speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for months, and lately I get blurry vision and headaches.” If the clinician asks what else is going on, volunteer: short of breath on stairs, several nosebleeds harder to stop, and ~8 lb unintentional weight loss with low appetite.

Only share these if specifically asked: tingling/numb hands/feet, dizziness on standing, hazy vision during headaches, easy bruising, lingering sinus/chest infections, night sweats a few times/week, and fullness on the left belly after small meals.

Do not mention unless directly prompted: fingers turning pale/blue and painful in cold, bleeding gums, mental “fog” on bad headache days, muffled hearing.

Express misconceptions: blame stress/poor sleep, call headaches “migraines,” attribute tingling to back/pinched nerves, nosebleeds to dry air/allergies.

For costs, assume Medicare fully covers ibrutinib; feel embarrassed about assistance; admit you might take it every other day if it’s expensive.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and clear. correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar lesion) with hypopituitarism and possible central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Central diabetes insipidus due to suprasellar mass
required_evidence: Clinical symptoms consistent with pituitary mass effect and/or hypopituitarism (e.g., headaches, visual symptoms, fatigue, low libido, amenorrhea/ED) and/or polyuria/polydipsia
Abnormal pituitary axis labs consistent with central adrenal insufficiency (low 8 AM cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH) and/or central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with low/normal TSH)
Water balance labs suggesting central diabetes insipidus (high/normal serum osmolality with inappropriately low urine osmolality and possible hypernatremia, low urine sodium in dehydration)
Imaging evidence of a sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion consistent with dermoid/epidermoid (MRI characteristics; epidermoid typically diffusion restriction; dermoid often T1 hyperintense due to fat)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Normal to high; may be elevated (hypernatremia) if significant free-water loss from central diabetes insipidus
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High-normal to elevated if central diabetes insipidus is present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately low/dilute (<300 mOsm/kg; often <100–200) in central diabetes insipidus despite elevated serum osmolality
expected_lab_results.Urine sodium: Often low in dehydration from water loss; may be variable depending on volume status and intake
expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (suggesting central adrenal insufficiency) or indeterminate; must be interpreted with ACTH and clinical context
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal in central adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism (not elevated despite low free T4)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low (central hypothyroidism) or low-normal with symptoms

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO on waking and 5 mg PO mid-afternoon (or equivalent physiologic glucocorticoid replacement) if central adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed
Hydrocortisone 50-100 mg IV q6-8h if acutely ill, perioperative, or adrenal crisis concern (stress-dose protocol as clinically indicated)
Levothyroxine 1.2-1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated/ensured) for central hypothyroidism; titrate to free T4 in upper half of reference range
Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO BID (or 10 mcg intranasal daily to BID) for symptomatic central diabetes insipidus; titrate to thirst/urine output and sodium
non_pharmacological: Urgent endocrinology referral for full pituitary evaluation and dynamic testing as needed (e.g., cosyntropin stimulation if indeterminate cortisol)

Neurosurgery referral for management of sellar/suprasellar dermoid/epidermoid cyst (surgical resection/decompression if symptomatic mass effect, visual compromise, hydrocephalus, or progressive endocrine deficits)
Ophthalmology/visual field testing if headaches, visual complaints, or suprasellar extension suspected
Fluid management: allow free access to water; monitor intake/output; avoid rapid correction of sodium; educate on desmopressin dosing to prevent hyponatremia
Perioperative planning: stress-dose steroids, careful sodium/osmolality monitoring, and postoperative monitoring for transient/worsening diabetes insipidus or SIADH
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks with endocrinology (sooner/urgent if severe symptoms, hypernatremia, suspected adrenal insufficiency, or visual symptoms); repeat sodium/osmolality and free T4 in 4-6 weeks after thyroid therapy changes; close perioperative follow-up if surgery planned

must_include: A cystic growth near the pituitary can disrupt hormone production and water balance
Dermoid/epidermoid cysts are typically benign but can cause symptoms by pressure or inflammation
Why the current labs suggest pituitary hormone deficiencies and/or central diabetes insipidus
Need for MRI characterization and specialist evaluation; urgency if visual symptoms or severe electrolyte abnormalities
If adrenal insufficiency is possible, steroids must be started before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
How to take hydrocortisone (split dosing), sick-day rules, and when to use stress dosing/emergency care
Levothyroxine in central hypothyroidism is monitored by free T4 (TSH is unreliable)
Desmopressin use, target symptoms (thirst/urine output), and hyponatremia warning signs (headache, nausea, confusion)
Red flags requiring urgent care: severe headache, vision changes, vomiting, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down
Possible need for surgery and expected goals (decompression, symptom control, preventing progression)
Medication side effects and what to do if doses are missed
Maintain access to water and track urine output if polyuria is present
Avoid over-restricting or over-consuming fluids when on desmopressin; follow individualized guidance
Carry medical alert identification for adrenal insufficiency and keep an emergency steroid plan
Follow-up testing schedule and importance of adherence

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms and red flags (headache, visual changes, polyuria/polydipsia, nausea/vomiting, confusion), timeline, medications, pregnancy status, and past endocrine/neurosurgical history to frame urgency and likely pituitary dysfunction.

Order initial endocrine and electrolyte evaluation to assess pituitary hormone deficiencies and possible central diabetes insipidus (DI) and to identify urgent abnormalities (e.g., sodium derangements).
Interpret labs to support suspected central adrenal insufficiency, central hypothyroidism, and/or central DI; triage urgency based on sodium, cortisol, and clinical stability.

If not already included or results equivocal, order confirmatory/adjunct tests (e.g., paired serum/urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, ACTH, repeat AM cortisol, prolactin, IGF-1, gonadotropins, pregnancy test when relevant) before definitive hormone titration.

Synthesize findings as suspected pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst with hypopituitarism and/or central DI; note need for MRI characterization and urgent referral if visual symptoms or severe electrolyte abnormalities.
Required safety check before prescribing any medications (e.g., hydrocortisone, levothyroxine, desmopressin).

Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., levothyroxine with calcium/iron, glucocorticoids with diabetes meds/NSAIDs, desmopressin with SSRIs/thiazides increasing hyponatremia risk).
Re-check allergies if additional meds are added later in the encounter or if initial list was incomplete.
Re-check interactions if medication plan changes (e.g., adding antiemetic, analgesic, or adjusting endocrine replacement).

Initiate appropriate hormone replacement sequence: if adrenal insufficiency is possible, start hydrocortisone first; then levothyroxine for central hypothyroidism (monitor free T4); consider desmopressin for confirmed/suspected central DI with careful sodium monitoring and patient education.

Required contraindication check before ordering: ensure ordering is appropriate/safe (e.g., pregnancy test before certain imaging pathways; repeat sodium/osmolality monitoring after desmopressin; avoid unsafe provocative testing in unstable patients).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment sequencing and monitoring, red flags, medication use and side effects, lifestyle guidance (fluids, urine tracking), and emergency steroid planning.

Arrange urgent MRI pituitary with contrast and endocrinology/neurosurgery/ophthalmology referral as indicated; schedule lab follow-up (free T4, electrolytes/sodium, morning cortisol/ACTH as appropriate) and safety check-ins.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and ensuring follow-up/urgent escalation plan.

patient.instructions:

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: IgM monoclonal gammopathy with hyperviscosity syndrome secondary to Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia
required_evidence: Neurologic symptom consistent with IgM-related hyperviscosity or neuropathy (ataxia)
Elevated total protein with low/normal albumin suggesting increased globulin fraction
Anemia pattern consistent with marrow involvement/chronic disease (low hemoglobin/hematocrit)

Need for confirmatory testing: serum protein electrophoresis/immunofixation showing IgM monoclonal spike and bone marrow biopsy showing lymphoplasmacytic infiltration (not in provided labs but required for definitive diagnosis)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal (normocytic) or mildly elevated; often normocytic anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal or mildly low/variable; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal or low (can be reduced with marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Globulin: High (elevated gamma globulins from monoclonal IgM)

medications: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 IV weekly x 4 doses (initiate after or with measures to reduce hyperviscosity if symptomatic)
Bendamustine 90 mg/m^2 IV days 1-2 every 28 days x 4-6 cycles (in combination with rituximab for symptomatic disease)
non_pharmacological: If symptomatic hyperviscosity suspected (e.g., ataxia with high globulin/total protein): urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and avoid delaying for chemotherapy initiation
Refer to hematology/oncology for definitive workup (SPEP/IFE, quantitative IgM, serum viscosity as indicated, bone marrow biopsy, MYD88 L265P testing) and treatment selection
Supportive care: transfuse packed RBCs if symptomatic anemia or hemodynamically significant anemia; avoid unnecessary RBC transfusion before plasmapheresis if hyperviscosity suspected
Fall-risk precautions and neurologic assessment given ataxia; consider PT/OT for gait stability
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Urgent hematology evaluation within 24-72 hours; same-day evaluation if neurologic symptoms suggest hyperviscosity. Reassess symptoms and CBC/chemistry within 1-2 weeks after initiating therapy or sooner if worsening.

must_include: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a slow-growing blood/lymph marrow cancer that makes excess IgM antibody
High IgM/protein can thicken blood (hyperviscosity) and affect the nervous system, which can contribute to symptoms like ataxia
Diagnosis requires confirmatory tests (SPEP/IFE, IgM level, bone marrow biopsy) beyond the basic labs
If hyperviscosity is suspected, plasma exchange is used urgently to remove IgM and improve neurologic symptoms
Disease-directed therapy commonly uses anti-CD20 therapy (rituximab) often combined with chemotherapy such as bendamustine for symptomatic disease
Treatment goals: symptom control, reduce IgM burden, improve blood counts, and prevent complications
Review warning signs requiring urgent care: worsening balance/vision changes, new confusion, bleeding, severe headache, shortness of breath
Discuss common treatment risks (infusion reactions with rituximab, infection risk, cytopenias) and the need for monitoring

Fall prevention strategies due to ataxia (assistive devices, home safety)

Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and lab monitoring during therapy

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male forklift operator on light duty. You speak clearly but are anxious and worried about losing your job/insurance, so you initially downplay severity and focus on “just getting older/out of shape” or a “pinched nerve.” Start by volunteering: legs gradually weaker for 2 years, worse climbing stairs/rising from low chairs, more tripping, ankles don’t lift (foot slap), and you tire faster walking. Mention low back aching and heavy legs; wonder about thyroid because of fatigue/dry skin; blame shoes.

Only if asked, add: weaker hands (jars/keys), occasional choking/cough with water, voice weaker/hoarse by day’s end, short of breath lying flat or unrefreshing sleep, smaller shoulders/hunched posture.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked about heart/sleep/falls: brief racing/skipping heartbeat, morning headaches/fog, using hands on thighs to stand, calves look bulky vs thighs, worse falls in dark/uneven ground.

Resist discussing sex and nighttime panic/gasping; admit reluctantly if asked directly and reassured.

Express cost concerns: PT “optional,” genetic/specialist tests “luxury.” If clinician shows empathy, you become more honest and ask what’s serious and what you can afford.

medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
Lisinopril 10 mg daily
allergies: Succinylcholine (history of severe hyperkalemia and prolonged weakness after anesthesia)
confounders: Statin-associated myopathy risk (on atorvastatin) with elevated CK
Suspected obstructive sleep apnea causing chronic hypercapnia and dyspnea, complicating assessment of neuromuscular respiratory weakness
Age-related deconditioning/overweight contributing to exertional intolerance
Mildly elevated AST from skeletal muscle injury can be misattributed to hepatic disease

correct_diagnosis.primary: Myofibrillar myopathy
acceptable_alternatives: Inherited muscular dystrophy (e.g., limb-girdle muscular dystrophy)
required_evidence: Progressive skeletal muscle weakness (often proximal and/or distal) with myopathic pattern on exam/history
Chronically elevated muscle enzymes (CK and/or aldolase) consistent with myopathy
Evidence of muscle injury contributing to mild AST/ALT elevation with otherwise non-hepatic pattern
Clinical context compatible with a chronic myopathy rather than acute rhabdomyolysis
History of severe hyperkalemia and prolonged weakness after anesthesia consistent with susceptibility to depolarizing neuromuscular blocker complications (supports safety constraints in myopathy)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Mild to moderately elevated chronically (may fluctuate); not typically thousands unless acute rhabdomyolysis
expected_lab_results.AST: Mildly elevated proportional to muscle injury; may exceed ALT
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (less than AST if muscle source predominates)
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Usually normal (helps distinguish skeletal muscle enzyme elevation from acute myocardial injury)
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Normal to mildly elevated; may be elevated if cardiomyopathy/heart failure involvement is present
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless reduced muscle mass (can be low) or renal impairment from other causes
expected_lab_results.eGFR: Typically normal unless comorbid kidney disease

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Avoid succinylcholine (contraindicated due to prior severe hyperkalemia and prolonged weakness)
non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist referral for confirmation/phenotyping and genetic counseling/testing as appropriate
Physical therapy with individualized low-to-moderate intensity strengthening and stretching; avoid overexertion that provokes prolonged weakness
Occupational therapy for energy conservation, adaptive equipment, and fall-risk mitigation
Cardiac screening and surveillance (ECG and echocardiography; consider ambulatory monitoring if palpitations/syncope) given potential cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia in myofibrillar myopathies
Pulmonary function assessment if respiratory symptoms, sleep-disordered breathing symptoms, or advanced weakness (e.g., FVC, nocturnal oximetry as indicated)

Perioperative planning: anesthesia consult; use non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers with careful monitoring; consider malignant hyperthermia-like precautions based on institutional protocol and patient history

must_include: Myofibrillar myopathy is a chronic muscle disease that causes progressive weakness

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a very anxious but clear-speaking parent of a 2-year-old girl. Start with: “My baby has had nonstop diarrhea and isn’t gaining weight.” Volunteer early: watery diarrhea many times daily since early infancy, poor weight gain despite frequent feeds, tired/weak most days, and you were told she has a prominent forehead. If asked, add: stools sometimes greasy/foul-smelling, frequent spit-up/vomiting after feeds, swollen belly/gas, recurrent fevers/infections, recent severe lung infection treated as staph pneumonia, flat midface, thin/breaking hair, and she dehydrates quickly (dry mouth, fewer wet diapers). Do not volunteer unless directly asked: you’ve been concentrating formula more and more, it worsens diarrhea; past labs showed sodium/potassium “off”; diaper rash; easy bruising/bleeding; delayed milestones.
Withhold initially (admit only with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning): you sometimes dilute formula to make it last longer and you’ve missed follow-ups because you felt judged. Express misconceptions: suspect milk allergy/lactose intolerance, celiac disease, or recurring daycare stomach bugs. Emphasize cost worries and ask to minimize tests/visits. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask for help with coverage/resources.

medications: Cefazolin 50 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO q6h PRN fever
Oral rehydration solution 50-100 mL/kg over 4 hours PRN dehydration
Multivitamin pediatric 1 mL PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
Elemental iron (ferrous sulfate) 3 mg/kg/day PO daily
confounders: Acute bacterial pneumonia with systemic inflammation causing transient cholestasis and elevated transaminases
Chronic diarrhea-related dehydration and electrolyte losses mimicking primary metabolic disorders
Protein-calorie malnutrition and micronutrient deficiencies contributing to poor growth and anemia
Possible secondary hypogammaglobulinemia from protein-losing enteropathy complicating infection risk assessment
Recent antibiotic exposure potentially causing antibiotic-associated diarrhea and altered stool inflammatory markers

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trichohepatoenteric syndrome (syndromic diarrhea; TTC37/SKIV2L-related)
acceptable_alternatives: Syndromic congenital diarrhea due to TTC37/SKIV2L (trichohepatoenteric syndrome spectrum)
Congenital diarrheal disorder with dysmorphic features (pending genetic confirmation)
required_evidence: Characteristic dysmorphic features (e.g., prominent forehead, midface retrusion) with suspected syndromic congenital diarrhea
History of chronic/intractable diarrhea beginning in infancy with failure to thrive or poor weight gain
Evidence of hair abnormality (woolly/fragile hair, trichorrhexis nodosa) on exam or microscopy (if obtained)
Hepatic involvement (hepatomegaly, elevated transaminases, cholestasis) or history suggestive of liver disease
Exclusion of common infectious causes of persistent diarrhea when clinically indicated
Genetic confirmation with biallelic pathogenic variants in TTC37 or SKIV2L (or plan to obtain testing)

expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low or normal (microcytosis if iron deficiency; may be normal early)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal or elevated if intercurrent infection; may be normal at baseline
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils (absolute): Normal or elevated if infection; neutropenia is not a defining feature

expected_lab_results.CRP: Often normal at baseline; elevated if intercurrent infection/inflammation
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Typically low unless bacterial sepsis is present
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: May be low-normal or low if significant diarrheal sodium losses/dehydration; normal if well-hydrated

Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 400–1000 IU PO daily (dose individualized to age/levels)

Elemental iron 3 mg/kg/day PO in 1–2 divided doses (if iron deficiency suspected/confirmed)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric gastroenterology and clinical genetics for confirmation (TTC37/SKIV2L testing) and comprehensive management
Nutrition support: high-calorie diet; early dietitian involvement; consider enteral tube feeding if inadequate intake; consider parenteral nutrition if severe malabsorption/intractable diarrhea
Monitor hydration status, growth parameters, and stool output closely; assess for dehydration and electrolyte derangements
Screen for and treat micronutrient deficiencies (iron, zinc, fat-soluble vitamins) and anemia; monitor liver function (transaminases, bilirubin) given risk of hepatopathy
Infection vigilance and immunization review; evaluate for immunodeficiency if recurrent/severe infections
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks with pediatric gastroenterology/dietitian (sooner within 24–72 hours if dehydration, poor intake, or worsening diarrhea); ongoing close growth and lab monitoring every 4–8 weeks initially

must_include: This is a rare inherited syndrome that can cause chronic diarrhea, poor growth, and distinctive facial/hair features
Diagnosis typically requires specialist evaluation and genetic testing (TTC37/SKIV2L)
Symptoms can fluctuate and complications can include dehydration, nutrient deficiencies, and possible liver involvement
Main goals are hydration, nutrition optimization, and monitoring for deficiencies and liver issues
Some children require tube feeds or parenteral nutrition depending on severity
When to seek urgent care: signs of dehydration (reduced urine, lethargy), blood in stool, persistent vomiting, fever, or worsening condition

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit nephritis-relevant history: edema, urine changes, hematuria/foamy urine, oliguria, HTN symptoms, recent infections (strep/skin), autoimmune symptoms (rash, arthralgias, oral ulcers), medication/toxin exposures (NSAIDs, supplements), volume status, pregnancy status if applicable, and red flags (SOB, chest pain, confusion).

Order initial nephritis/AKI workup: BMP (Cr/BUN/K/HCO3), CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio (or ACR), urine sodium/FeNa if helpful, albumin, lipids if nephrotic features, complements (C3/C4), ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, ASO/anti-DNase B if post-strep suspected, hepatitis B/C, HIV; consider renal ultrasound if obstruction/size concerns.
Interpret kidney function (Cr/BUN/eGFR), electrolyte/acid-base status (K, HCO3), and urine findings (RBCs, casts, protein) to assess severity, likely glomerular process, and urgency.

Synthesize findings to diagnose suspected nephritis (e.g., glomerulonephritis pattern) and stratify severity (AKI, nephritic vs nephrotic features, hypertensive emergency, hyperkalemia/metabolic acidosis).
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm allergy list and reaction details (especially penicillin) to guide antibiotic choices if infection treatment becomes necessary.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions with current meds/supplements (including testosterone, NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, diuretics, anticoagulants) and nephrotoxins.

Initiate indicated supportive therapy (e.g., diuretic for volume overload, potassium binder for hyperkalemia, sodium bicarbonate for significant metabolic acidosis) and avoid nephrotoxins; ensure dosing is kidney-adjusted and aligned with contraindications.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment goals/side effects/monitoring and possible biopsy/hospitalization, address concerns (penicillin allergy and testosterone), and lifestyle guidance (low sodium, fluid guidance, avoid NSAIDs/supplements, home BP/weights).

Arrange close follow-up with repeat labs (BMP, urine protein, UA) within appropriate timeframe (hours–days depending on severity) and nephrology referral; include clear return precautions and escalation plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Asks about key nephritis symptoms: hematuria (tea-colored urine), foamy urine/proteinuria, edema, reduced urine output, and new/worsening hypertension.

Screens for red flags requiring urgent care or ED referral: markedly reduced urine output/anuria, shortness of breath/pulmonary edema, chest pain, confusion/uremia symptoms, severe swelling, severe headache/vision changes suggestive of hypertensive emergency.

Elicits potential triggers/causes: recent pharyngitis/skin infection, systemic autoimmune symptoms (rash, arthralgias, oral ulcers), vasculitis symptoms (sinus/lung bleeding), new medications (NSAIDs, antibiotics), and OTC/herbal supplements.

Orders and interprets appropriate diagnostic tests: BMP (Cr/BUN/eGFR, K, HCO3), UA with microscopy (RBCs, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts), quantification of proteinuria (UPCR/ACR), and immune/infectious labs (C3/C4, ANA/dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis/HIV, ASO/anti-DNase B when indicated).
Explains diagnosis in patient-friendly language, explicitly stating kidney inflammation affecting glomeruli can reduce filtering and kidney function.
Explains what available labs suggest (changes in creatinine/BUN/eGFR) and why additional urine and immune testing is needed to determine the cause and guide treatment.
Explains treatment goals: protect kidney function, control blood pressure/volume status, and correct dangerous electrolytes/acid-base issues.
If prescribing, performs critical safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check; does not prescribe until these are completed.
Performs drug interaction checking before prescribing when feasible; documents rationale if omitted due to urgency.
Uses kidney-appropriate medication choices and dosing considerations; avoids or cautions against nephrotoxins (NSAIDs) and inappropriate agents in AKI (e.g., unadjusted renally cleared drugs).

When discussing medications, states purpose and key side effects: diuretics (dehydration, low BP), potassium binders (GI effects/constipation), bicarbonate (bloating, alkalosis) and need for monitoring.
Addresses penicillin allergy history (reaction type/timing) and explains how it affects antibiotic choices if infection treatment becomes necessary.
Addresses testosterone use, noting potential BP/fluid retention effects; offers shared decision-making about holding vs continuing based on severity and clinician oversight.
Sets expectations about prognosis depending on cause and speed of treatment; avoids false reassurance and emphasizes uncertainty until workup complete.

Provides lifestyle counseling: low-sodium diet, individualized fluid guidance based on edema/BP, avoidance of NSAIDs and potentially harmful OTC supplements, and home monitoring (daily weights, BP if possible).

Arranges close follow-up and repeat labs; escalates to urgent evaluation/hospitalization for severe AKI, hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, pulmonary edema, hypertensive emergency, or rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis concern.
Uses clear safety-net instructions and checks understanding (teach-back) before ending the encounter.

minimal_paths.description: Stable suspected nephritis without immediate life-threatening complications; complete diagnostic workup, start supportive care if indicated, educate, and arrange close follow-up/nephrology referral.
steps: ASK focused nephritis history and red flags
ORDER_LAB nephritis/AKI panel including UA microscopy and protein quantification plus complements/autoimmune serologies as indicated
GET_RESULTS and assess severity (Cr trend/eGFR, K, HCO3, UA findings)
DIAGNOSE suspected glomerulonephritis/nephritis and stratify risk
CHECK_INTERACTION prior to prescribing when possible
PRESCRIBE supportive therapy if needed (e.g., diuretic for edema, K binder for mild-moderate hyperkalemia, bicarbonate for significant acidosis) with kidney-appropriate dosing
EDUCATE diagnosis, treatment goals/side effects/monitoring, address penicillin allergy and testosterone concerns, lifestyle guidance, and urgent warning signs
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP within 24-72h (or sooner based on labs) with repeat BMP/UA/UPCR and nephrology referral

minimal_paths.description: Suspected nephritis with severe features suggesting urgent ED/hospital evaluation (e.g., rapidly rising creatinine, hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, pulmonary edema, hypertensive emergency, or RPGN concern).
steps: ASK targeted red flags and severity symptoms
ORDER_LAB urgent BMP and UA microscopy (and ECG if hyperkalemia suspected, if available in setting)
GET_RESULTS and identify emergent abnormalities (K elevation, low HCO3, marked Cr rise, heavy hematuria/proteinuria)
DIAGNOSE suspected severe nephritis/RPGN or nephritic syndrome with complications
EDUCATE on urgent warning signs and need for ED/hospitalization; explain possible need for IV therapies, biopsy, and close monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP as immediate escalation (ED now) and nephrology involvement

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders renal ultrasound early to exclude obstruction or assess kidney size before finalizing diagnosis; still completes UA microscopy and immune workup.

If no prescribing is needed (mild findings, no edema/hyperkalemia/acidosis), agent focuses on workup, nephrotoxin avoidance, and close monitoring; still performs allergy check only if considering future antibiotics or documents deferral appropriately.

Agent performs contraindication reasoning explicitly within prescribing step (e.g., avoids ACEi/ARB initiation in active AKI or significant hyperkalemia) while still completing required checks before prescribing.
steps: PRESCRIBE (includes explicit contraindication screening and renal dosing)
